missions.title: System Grounding
missions.philosophy: Like Tai Chi, where you must feel the ground before moving, a programmer must understand their environment. 'Wu Wei' is achieved when your tools become extensions of your mind.
missions.economics: Infrastructure is the basis of all macro-economic stability. Before building goods, we build roads. Here, your terminal and shell are your economic infrastructure.
missions.tech_concept: The terminal. Git is our tool for interacting with the open-source global supply chain of ideas.
missions.challenge: Type the command to clone a git repository.

missions.title: Variables & Data Lineage
missions.philosophy: Genealogy teaches us that tracing lineage reveals identity. A variable is a named vessel; its lineage of data determines its behavior in the ecosystem.
missions.economics: Micro-economics dictates that resources are scarce. Variables allocate memory—our most precious digital resource. Good naming prevents systemic inflation.
missions.tech_concept: In Python, we assign values using the '=' operator. E.g., `water_supply = 100`.
missions.challenge: Write exactly this: assign the integer 42 to a variable named 'seed'.

missions.title: JSON — The Data Language
missions.philosophy: Dr. Cornel West reminds us that 'justice is what love looks like in public.' In our digital realm, interoperability is what cooperation looks like in code. JSON is that shared public language.
missions.economics: Global trade requires a universal currency standard. JSON acts as the fiat currency of data exchange across the web, allowing disparate systems to trade value.
missions.tech_concept: JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) structures data in key-value pairs, easily readable by humans and machines.
missions.challenge: What does JSON stand for? (Type out the 3 words)

missions.title: Functions & 'Jeet Kune Do' Logic
missions.philosophy: Bruce Lee's Jeet Kune Do emphasizes 'maximum efficiency with minimum effort.' Functions embody this: write once, strike (execute) infinitely.
missions.economics: A function is a social contract or a specialized factory. It takes raw materials (inputs/arguments), processes them through labor (logic), and outputs a finished good.
missions.tech_concept: Defined using 'def'. They encapsulate logic. `def plant_tree(type): return type + ' planted'`
missions.challenge: What keyword is used to define a function in Python?

missions.title: Git — Journalistic Integrity
missions.philosophy: Social activism requires truth. Git is our immutable journalistic ledger. It holds systems accountable by tracking exactly who changed what, and when.
missions.economics: The Baltic states, particularly Estonia, leverage cryptographic ledgers to ensure transparent e-governance. Git operates on a similar Merkle-tree foundation.
missions.tech_concept: Git commits are snapshots of history. We use `git commit -m 'message'` to log changes.
missions.challenge: What command stages all your current changes in Git? (Hint: git ___ .)

missions.title: Git — Branching & Merging
missions.philosophy: Wing Chun teaches us to deflect and redirect force rather than opposing it. A Git branch allows you to divert the flow of time, safely experimenting with alternate 'genealogical' lineages of code before merging them back to the main trunk.
missions.economics: In macro-economics, this is Research & Development (R&D). You isolate risk in a separate sandbox (branch) so experimental failures do not crash the primary market (main branch).
missions.tech_concept: We use branches to isolate work. `git checkout -b <name>` creates and moves to a new branch.
missions.challenge: What command would you use to create and switch to a new branch named 'feature'?

missions.title: APIs & HTTP — The Digital Journalist
missions.philosophy: Investigative journalism relies on primary sources. To hold systems accountable, we cannot wait to be spoon-fed data; we must query the source directly. APIs are the Freedom of Information Act of the digital world.
missions.economics: Information asymmetry causes market failure. Open APIs democratize data, allowing independent actors to build tools that correct corporate or governmental monopolies on knowledge.
missions.tech_concept: An API (Application Programming Interface) allows two programs to talk. In Python, we use the `requests` library to GET data from URLs.
missions.challenge: What standard Python library is used to make HTTP requests to web APIs?

missions.title: File I/O — Institutional Memory
missions.philosophy: The Bushido code values legacy. Without memory, a society cannot learn. Writing data to a file ensures that your digital actions echo into the future, much like Estonia's resilient national data registry.
missions.economics: Capital accumulation requires storage. If a program loses all its data when it closes, it cannot generate generational wealth. File I/O is the banking system of our program.
missions.tech_concept: Python uses `open('file.txt', 'w')` to write to a file. The `with` keyword ensures the file is safely closed when we are done.
missions.challenge: What Python keyword is used to open a file and ensure it closes automatically?

missions.title: OOP — Sociological Modeling
missions.philosophy: Theatre teaches us that a play requires Roles (scripts/blueprints) and Actors (individuals performing the role). Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) applies sociology to code: defining community blueprints so individual actors can interact.
missions.economics: A Class is a franchise business model (like the concept of a cooperative bakery). An Object is the actual physical bakery in your neighborhood, instantiated from that model.
missions.tech_concept: We use the `class` keyword to define these blueprints. Objects are created from them.
missions.challenge: What keyword do you use to define an object blueprint in Python?

missions.title: Code Review — The Gentle Art
missions.philosophy: Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu (BJJ) translates to 'the gentle art.' When we roll with our training partners, the goal is not destruction, but mutual growth. Code review is our digital tatami mat; we test each other's code to find weaknesses before the real world does.
missions.economics: Quality Assurance (QA) prevents negative externalities. Reviewing code ensures that technical debt is not offloaded onto the future community or end-users.
missions.tech_concept: On platforms like GitHub, we propose our code changes for review before they are merged into the main project.
missions.challenge: What is the two-word term for proposing a merge so peers can review your code on GitHub?
